title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Tyranny of Choice: Is More Always Better?
content.text: In modern Western societies, we are raised on the mantra that 'more choice equals more freedom.' From the dozens of cereal brands in the supermarket to the thousands of movies on streaming platforms, we have never had more options. Logic suggests this should make us happier. || (41) || [41] This phenomenon, known as the 'paradox of choice,' suggests that beyond a certain point, an abundance of options actually leads to anxiety rather than satisfaction [/41]. Psychologist Barry Schwartz argues that instead of liberating us, having too many choices tends to paralyze us. || (42) || [42] One reason for this is the psychological concept of 'opportunity cost' [/42]. Every time we choose one thing, we are implicitly rejecting all the other attractive alternatives. The more options there are, the easier it is to imagine that one of the rejected choices would have been better. || (43) || [43] This leads to a second problem: the escalation of expectations [/43]. When there are dozens of different pairs of jeans available, we expect to find the 'perfect' pair. If the ones we buy are anything less than flawless, we feel a sense of disappointment that wouldn't exist if our choices had been limited. || (44) || [44] Furthermore, this abundance of choice often leads to 'post-decision regret' [/44]. Even if we make an objectiveley good choice, we worry that it wasn't the absolute best. We find ourselves constantly looking over our shoulders, wondering 'what if.' || (45) || [45] To cope with this, Schwartz identifies two types of decision-makers: 'maximizers' and 'satisficers' [/45]. Maximizers are those who feel they must explore every single option before deciding, while satisficers look for something that meets their basic criteria and then stop. || (46) || [46] Unsurprisingly, research shows that satisficers tend to be significantly happier [/46]. By accepting 'good enough,' they avoid the mental exhaustion of the endless search and the regret that follows it. In an age of infinite options, the real freedom might lie in learning how to limit them.

paragraphs.A: [42] This mental block occurs because the effort required to compare all the variables becomes overwhelming [/42]. Faced with 50 types of jam, many consumers simply walk away without buying anything at all, a result that contradicts traditional economic theories.
paragraphs.F: [41] However, recent studies in behavioral economics have shown that the opposite is often true [/41]. As the number of options increases, the psychological burden of making a decision grows, leading to a decrease in overall well-being.
paragraphs.B: [44] It also shifts the blame for a bad decision from the world to ourselves [/44]. If there is only one type of bread available and it's dry, it’s the baker’s fault. If there are a hundred types and you pick a bad one, you feel it is entirely your own failure.
paragraphs.D: [43] In a world of few options, we are pleasantly surprised when something exceeds our modest hopes [/43]. But when options are endless, our standards rise to impossible levels, ensuring that we are never truly content with our selection.
paragraphs.E: [46] The former, however, often achieve better objective results but feel worse about them [/46]. Because they are haunted by the 'perfect' choice they might have missed, their success is bittersweet.
paragraphs.C: [45] These two strategies represent opposite ends of the psychological spectrum [/45]. One leads to an exhaustive and often draining search for perfection, while the other prioritizes peace of mind over optimal outcomes.
paragraphs.G: Marketing experts have used this knowledge to simplify their product lines. By offering a 'good, better, best' tier, they help customers make decisions faster and with more confidence.

questions.explanation: F introduces the contrast with the 'logic' of the initial text, mentioning studies that contradict the idea that more choice is better.
A explains the 'paralysis' mentioned by Schwartz, using the example of the 50 types of jam.
D connects with the concept of expectations, comparing the world of few options with that of infinite options.
B adds the factor of one's own 'guilt' to the decision process, complementing the point about regret.
C describes the two strategies (maximizers/satisficers) before the text explains which of the two is happier.
E refers to 'the former' (the maximizers), explaining why they feel worse despite obtaining better results.
